truth.papers.A54.title: Effect of temperature on the oxidation of Fe-7.5Al-0.65C alloy
truth.papers.A54.paper_scope: Investigates the effect of temperature (600–900°C) on the oxidation behavior of Fe-7.5Al-0.65C alloy in dry air, focusing on kinetics, oxide scale morphology, internal oxidation, and the stability of (Fe3AlCx) carbide (K-phase).

prediction.papers.A54.title: Effect of temperature on the oxidation of Fe-7.5Al-0.65C alloy
prediction.papers.A54.paper_scope: Investigate the effect of temperature on the oxidation behaviour of Fe-7.5Al-0.65C alloy between 600 and 900°C in dry air, focusing on oxidation kinetics, oxide scale morphology, internal oxidation mechanisms, and the role of aluminium diffusion and carbide stability.

truth.alloys.alloy_fe7.5al0.65c.alloys_notes: Actual composition from Table I; carbon content 0.65 wt%. The alloy is referred to as Fe-7.5Al-0.65C throughout the paper.

truth.structures.sample_fe7.5al0.65c_as_hom.ov: Two-phase microstructure: ferrite matrix (α-Fe) with iron-aluminium carbide (Fe3AlCx) particles (K-phase).
truth.structures.sample_fe7.5al0.65c_ox600_24h: Base alloy ferrite + carbide, covered by oxide scale with nodules and internal oxidation.
substrate (ferrite+carbide) + outer Fe2O3 + middle (FeAl)2O3 + inner internal Al-oxide
fine internal Al-oxide precipitates beneath nodules
truth.structures.sample_fe7.5al0.65c_ox600_96h: Similar to 24 h oxidation at 600°C: substrate with ferrite+carbide, scale with Fe2O3/(FeAl)2O3/internal Al-oxide, but thicker/longer oxidation. No decarburization.
substrate + outer Fe2O3 + middle (FeAl)2O3 + inner internal Al-oxide
outer nodule layer (Fe-rich, 61-68 wt% Fe, 0% Al)
inner region, Al-rich (up to 17.65 wt%), Fe 73.8-83.4 wt%
truth.structures.sample_fe7.5al0.65c_ox700_24h: Substrate with ferrite+carbide, many nodules, no internal oxidation, no decarburization.
substrate + outer porous iron oxide + inner Al-enriched layer
outer porous layer with uniform iron content
truth.structures.sample_fe7.5al0.65c_ox800_24h: Few small nodules; Al2O3 on surface; substrate unchanged.
truth.structures.sample_fe7.5al0.65c_ox900_24h: No nodules, no internal oxidation, no decarburization; continuous protective alumina scale.

prediction.structures.sample_fe7.5al0.65c_as_hom.ov: Two-phase material consisting of ferrite matrix with iron-aluminium carbide (Fe3AlCx) particles
prediction.structures.sample_fe7.5al0.65c_ox600_24h: Surface nodules with multilayer oxide scale and internal oxidation beneath nodules; no decarburization
prediction.structures.sample_fe7.5al0.65c_ox700_24h: Nodules on surface with outer porous iron-rich layer and inner aluminium-rich layer; no internal oxidation and no decarburization
outer porous layer of uniform iron content
prediction.structures.sample_fe7.5al0.65c_ox800_24h: Few small nodules and Al2O3 on surface
prediction.structures.sample_fe7.5al0.65c_ox900_24h: Continuous protective oxide scale with no nodules; no internal oxidation and no decarburization

truth.interfaces.sample_fe7.5al0.65c_ox600_24h: Internal Al-oxide precipitates formed beneath nodules due to oxygen inward diffusion through pores/microcracks in Fe2O3; no carbide-free layer observed.

At 600°C, initial parabolic oxidation followed by accelerated attack; nodules of Fe2O3/(FeAl)2O3 develop, and internal oxidation of Al occurs because the Al flux is insufficient to form a continuous protective scale. Carbide remains stable.
truth.interfaces.sample_fe7.5al0.65c_ox700_24h: Internal oxidation suppressed; healing Al2O3 or FeAl2O4 layer forms beneath nodules, acting as barrier to outward Fe cation diffusion.
At 700°C, many nodules grow but a healing layer arrests internal oxidation; overall rate is higher than at 800-900°C because of nodule formation.
truth.interfaces.sample_fe7.5al0.65c_ox800_24h: At 800°C, increased Al mobility rapidly suppresses nodule growth; few small nodules form, and a spinel or alumina layer dominates.
truth.interfaces.sample_fe7.5al0.65c_ox900_24h: At 900°C, the Al flux is sufficient to establish a stable, continuous Al2O3 scale; no nodules or internal oxidation are observed. Final weight gain is the lowest among all temperatures.
truth.interfaces.sample_fe7.5al0.65c_ox600_96h: Same mechanism as 600°C/24 h, but after 96 h the extent of internal oxidation and scale thickness is greater. No decarburization.
Prolonged exposure at 600°C enhances internal oxidation and nodule growth; no carbide decomposition detected.

prediction.interfaces.sample_fe7.5al0.65c_ox600_24h: Internal oxidation beneath nodules prevents aluminium diffusion to the nodule–alloy interface; CO/CO2 transport via pores and microcracks provides rapid oxygen diffusion channels.
prediction.interfaces.sample_fe7.5al0.65c_ox700_24h: A healing layer of Al2O3 or FeAl2O4 forms and acts as a barrier to outward diffusion of iron cations, suppressing internal oxidation ahead of growing nodules.
prediction.interfaces.sample_fe7.5al0.65c_ox900_24h: Sufficient aluminium flux ensures stability of spinel or alumina scale, reducing oxygen activity and inhibiting internal oxidation; carbide decomposition along oxide–matrix interface becomes ineffective.

truth.properties.sample_fe7.5al0.65c_ox600_24h: dry air, flow rate 100 ml/min, heating/cooling 100°C/min

Kp1 (initial parabolic, <4 h): 2.4×10^-10 g^2 cm^-4 sec^-1 (text value; Table II lists 2.4×10^-12). No Kp2 because the rate does not follow a simple parabolic law after ~4 h; oxidation resistance decreases.
Initial parabolic kinetics, then dramatic increase in oxidation rate; nodules and internal oxidation develop.
None protective; Fe2O3/(FeAl)2O3 nodules with internal Al-oxide beneath.
truth.properties.sample_fe7.5al0.65c_ox600_96h: Long-term (96 h) exposure at 600°C continues internal oxidation and nodule growth; no protective scale formation.
None; thick, layered Fe2O3/(FeAl)2O3 scale with internal Al-oxide.
truth.properties.sample_fe7.5al0.65c_ox700_24h: Two distinct parabolic rates: Kp1 (<4 h): 6.4×10^-11 g^2 cm^-4 sec^-1; Kp2 (>14 h): 2.2×10^-12 g^2 cm^-4 sec^-1.
Rapid initial weight gain, then steady state after healing layer formation; final plateau.
Outer porous iron oxide, inner Al-enriched layer (healing layer suppresses internal oxidation).
truth.properties.sample_fe7.5al0.65c_ox800_24h: Oxidation rate decreases with time; few small nodules form; predominantly protective behavior.
Al2O3 on surface with few small Fe-oxide nodules.
truth.properties.sample_fe7.5al0.65c_ox900_24h: Lowest final weight gain; oxidation rate gradually decreases to a plateau; no nodules.
Stable continuous Al2O3 scale; no decarburization.

truth.characterization_methods.sample_fe7.5al0: Phase identification in the as-homogenized alloy
Two phases: ferrite (α-Fe, bcc, a=0.287 nm) and iron-aluminium carbide Fe3AlCx (a=0.378 nm).
Elemental distribution and quantitative point analysis across oxide scale
Outer layer Fe2O3 (Fe ~61-68%, Al 0%), middle (FeAl)2O3 (Fe ~50-57%, Al ~12-16%), inner Al-rich internal oxide. Similar layers found after 96 h (see Table III).

prediction.characterization_methods.sample_fe7.5al0: Two-phase structure with dark Fe3AlCx particles in light ferrite matrix
elemental line profile and X-ray mapping
Outer porous layer of uniform iron content and inner layer enriched with aluminium
Few small nodules and Al2O3 on surface
No nodules or internal oxidation; continuous oxide scale present

truth.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: The paper states that the parabolic rate constant Kp1 at 600°C is 2.4×10^-10 g^2 cm^-4 sec^-1 in the text, while Table II lists 2.4×10^-12 g^2 cm^-4 sec^-1. The text value is used in the oxidation resistance property, but the discrepancy is noted.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: Thermogravimetric oxidation weight-gain data (Fig. 2, approximate values from published graph; units assumed mg/cm^2): At 600°C: 0 h (0), 5 h (0.3), 10 h (0.5), 15 h (0.7), 20 h (0.9), 25 h (1.1), 30 h (1.3). At 700°C: 0 h (0), 5 h (1.2), 10 h (1.4), 15 h (1.5), 20 h (1.5), 25 h (1.5), 30 h (1.5). At 800°C: 0 h (0), 5 h (0.2), 10 h (0.3), 15 h (0.4), 20 h (0.5), 25 h (0.6), 30 h (0.7). At 900°C: 0 h (0), 5 h (0.1), 10 h (0.2), 15 h (0.3), 20 h (0.4), 25 h (0.4), 30 h (0.4). Exact units not specified in caption.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: Proposed mechanism for nodule formation and internal oxidation at 600°C: Initially, Al2O3 nuclei cover part of the surface while FeO forms at random sites. CO/CO2 is produced from carbide decomposition at the interface and escapes through pores/microcracks, creating fast diffusion paths. Oxygen diffuses inward through FeO nodules, reacts with Al to form internal Al2O3 precipitates, depleting Al that would otherwise reach the interface and maintain a protective scale. This explains the non-simple parabolic kinetics.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: At 700°C and above, internal oxidation is suppressed by the formation of a healing Al2O3 or FeAl2O4 layer that blocks outward Fe cation diffusion. At 900°C, the Al flux is high enough to sustain a continuous Al2O3 scale, eliminating nodules entirely.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: The role of carbon (as K-phase carbide) on oxidation: At 600°C, carbide is stable and contributes to CO/CO2 evolution, accelerating oxidation. At higher temperatures, carbide decomposition at the interface becomes ineffective due to rapid Al diffusion and healing layer formation. No decarburization layer was observed at any condition.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: Hot forging at 1200°C from 7 cm to 2.5 cm thickness, followed by 1100°C/4 h homogenization, produced a ferrite matrix with dispersed (Fe3AlCx) carbide particles (K-phase), as shown in Fig. 1 (scale bar 40 μm; grain size not numerically reported).
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: XRD lattice parameters: ferrite a=0.287 nm, carbide a=0.378 nm.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: Table III provides quantitative EPMA point analysis of oxide layers after 6, 12, 24, 48, 96 h at 600°C: inner layer Fe 70.9-83.4 wt%, Al 9.0-17.65 wt%; middle layer Fe 49.9-57.2 wt%, Al 9.7-16 wt%; outer layer Fe 61-68.4 wt%, Al 0 wt%.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_8.value: The infrared image furnace used for TGA had a heating/cooling rate of 100°C/min, and dry air flowed at 100 ml/min.

prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: At 600°C, the oxidation kinetics do not obey a simple parabolic rate law within 24 h; the initial stage follows a parabolic rate, then a decrease in oxidation resistance is observed with dramatically increased oxidation.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: The kinetic results at 700, 800 and 900°C exhibit two distinct parabolic rates (Kp1 and Kp2). Kp1 increases with temperature from 700 to 900°C, whereas Kp2 decreases with increasing temperature over the same range.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: Table II parabolic rate constants summary: 600°C Kp1 = 2.4×10⁻¹² g² cm⁻⁴ sec⁻¹ (<4 h) and no Kp2; 700°C Kp1 = 6.4×10⁻¹¹ (<4 h), Kp2 = 2.2×10⁻¹² (>14 h); 800°C Kp1 = 4.9×10⁻¹² (<4 h), Kp2 = 0.6×10⁻¹² (>12 h); 900°C Kp1 = 5.6×10⁻¹² (<4 h), Kp2 = 1.0×10⁻¹³ (>10 h). Note: the main text states 2.4×10⁻¹⁰ for 600°C Kp1, which appears inconsistent with Table II and the kinetic trend in Fig. 2.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: Thermogravimetric data extracted from Fig. 2 (values read from table): at 700°C weight gain reached ~1.5 (arb. units) by 20 h and plateaued; at 600°C weight gain increased monotonically to ~1.3 at 30 h; at 800°C and 900°C weight gains were lower, reaching ~0.7 and ~0.4 respectively at 30 h. Exact y-axis units not stated in text.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: Table III EPMA point analysis of oxide layers on Fe-7.5Al-0.65C at 600°C shows iron and aluminium contents in inner, middle and outer layers for oxidation times of 6, 12, 24, 48 and 96 h; outer layer consistently contained 0 wt% Al, while inner layer ranged from 9.0–17.65 wt% Al depending on time.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: Fig. 5 concentration profile (line scan) across the oxide scale after 96 h at 600°C shows Fe content varying from ~67 wt% near the surface down to ~7.5 wt% in the matrix, with corresponding Al enrichment in the inner layer.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: Internal oxidation mechanism at 600°C: initial nuclei of Al2O3, FeO and CO (or CO2) form; CO/CO2 evaporates from the surface and also transports through scale pores and microcracks, providing rapid oxygen diffusion channels into the substrate where it reacts with aluminium to form dispersed alumina beneath nodules.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: At 700°C, internal oxidation ahead of growing nodules is suppressed by the formation of a healing layer of Al2O3 or FeAl2O4 which acts as a barrier to outward diffusion of iron cations; number of nodules decreases with increasing temperature.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_8.value: At 800°C, nodule growth is rapidly suppressed and size is small. At 900°C, nodule growth is not observed; aluminium flux is sufficient to ensure stability of a spinel layer or alumina oxide scale, reducing oxygen activity and inhibiting internal oxidation.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_9.value: The change in oxidation behaviour between 600°C and 700–900°C is attributed to the effect of temperature on aluminium diffusion in the alloy and on carbide (K-phase) stability; carbide is stable at 600°C but decomposition along the oxide–matrix interface becomes ineffective at 900°C.
